content:
# Judges Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_279.jpg, JDG_page_280.jpg

### Tola, Jair, and the Ammonite Crisis

1. After Abimelech died, Tola son of Puah and grandson of Dodo stepped up to rescue Israel. He was from the tribe of Issachar but lived in Shamir in the hill country of Ephraim.

2. He judged Israel for twenty-three years. Then he died and was buried in Shamir.

3. After him came Jair from Gilead, who judged Israel for twenty-two years.

4. He had thirty sons, who rode on thirty donkeys and controlled thirty towns in the land of Gilead, which are still called the Towns of Jair.

5. When Jair died, he was buried in Kamon.

6. Again the Israelites did evil in the LORD's sight. They served the Baals and the Ashtoreths, and the gods of Aram, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and Philistia. They abandoned the LORD and no longer served him at all.

7. So the LORD burned with anger against Israel, and he turned them over to the Philistines and the Ammonites,

8. who began to crush and oppress the Israelites that year. For eighteen years they oppressed all the Israelites east of the Jordan in the land of the Amorites (that is, in Gilead).

9. Then the Ammonites crossed the Jordan and attacked Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim, and Israel was in great distress.

10. Finally the Israelites cried out to the LORD for help, saying, "We have sinned against you because we have abandoned you as our God and have served the images of Baal."

11. The LORD replied, "Did I not rescue you from the Egyptians, the Amorites, the Ammonites, and the Philistines?

12. The Sidonians, the Amalekites, and the Maonites also oppressed you — and when you cried out to me, I rescued you from their power.

13. Yet you have abandoned me and served other gods. So I will not rescue you anymore.

14. Go and cry out to the gods you have chosen! Let them rescue you in your hour of distress!"

15. But the Israelites pleaded with the LORD and said, "We have sinned. Punish us as you see fit, only rescue us today from our enemies."

16. Then the Israelites put aside their foreign gods and served the LORD. And he was grieved by their misery.

17. At that time the Ammonite army was mobilized and camped in Gilead, and the people of Israel assembled and camped at Mizpah.

18. The leaders of Gilead said to each other, "Whoever is willing to lead the attack against the Ammonites will become ruler over all the people of Gilead."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The broken cycle (vv.10-16):** This is the most emotionally complex passage in Judges. God's response to Israel's plea is something new — a refusal. "I will not rescue you anymore. Go cry out to the gods you have chosen." It's devastating, and it breaks the predictable sin-cry-rescue pattern. But then something even more remarkable happens: Israel confesses, puts aside its idols, and submits to whatever God decides — "Punish us as you see fit, only rescue us today." And God relents. Not because the logic of justice demands it, but because "he was grieved by their misery." The tension between justice and mercy is the theological heart of the Hebrew Bible.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
